ascent_style.meaning: Weighting gear to make upward progress.
Modifier: no falls, no resting on gear.
Modifier: worked the route resting on gear (hangdog).
Lead first try, clean, with prior beta.
Attempted from the ground up, no top-rope rehearsal.
Trad: clean lead after top-rope practice.
Lead first try, clean, no prior beta.
Redpoint with gear pre-placed (now usually folded into redpoint).
Climbed after the leader, roped from above.
Climbed on a rope anchored above.

character.meaning: Balance/friction climbing; precision under little security.
Big-air positions beyond what the protection grade captures.
Significant small holds on the hard sections (Rockfax "f").
Bold, heady climbing — spaced or marginal gear above hard moves (the Rockfax heart symbol; UK climbers say "bold").
Steep endurance climbing — the pump is the crux.
Rounded, sloping holds and breaks (gritstone-style) — Rockfax symbol; friction- and humidity-sensitive.
Lots of hard moves with little respite (Rockfax "s").
Intricate movement; body position over pulling.

discipline.meaning: Multiple rope-lengths with belay stances (the platform's focus).
Easy ungraded/Grade 1-3 terrain between walking and climbing — approach ridges, easy alpine crests.

feature.meaning: A narrow outward-pointing edge or prow — climbed on or beside it, exposed on both sides.
A rounded steepening passed in a move or two — "pull through the bulge"; smaller than a roof.
A crack wide enough to fit the whole body inside; climbed by back-and-footing.
An inside angle where two faces meet (dihedral / open book) — the opposite of an arête.
A fissure taking jams and natural protection — the classic trad medium.
An open wall climbed on holds rather than a crack line.
A partly-detached sheet of rock forming an edge or crack behind it — check it is attached.
A shallow rounded corner or scoop, often climbed by bridging.
A wide mountain cleft or couloir line — common on UK/Irish mountain crags; often loose or wet.
A crack too wide to jam and too narrow to chimney — awkward, specialist technique and big gear.
A free-standing or semi-detached column of rock.
Holes or solution pockets used as holds (limestone / conglomerate).
An inclined ledge or weakness trending diagonally across steeper rock.
A long crest line with exposure on both sides — alpine-flavoured climbing.
A horizontal overhang crossed on its underside.
Rock below vertical — friction and balance rather than pulling.
Calcite ribs and blobs left by flowing water on limestone; pinched rather than pulled.

hazard.meaning: High enough for thin air / altitude effects.
Seasonal nesting restriction (BMC RAD-style) — the crag or route may be banned in spring/summer.
Glassy, traffic-polished rock — badly friction-dependent; treacherous when humid or damp (Rockfax condition note).
Exposed to lightning / no quick escape in a storm.

hazard.feeds: tide-window logic (live 2026-07-05: planner tide tiles, Open-Meteo Marine)

hazard.meaning: Sustained sideways climbing — complicates retreat, rope-work and seconding; kept as a hazard because it gates escapability.
Dirty or vegetated rock — needs traffic or a dry spell; slower to dry after rain.

protection_grade.meaning: Mostly good; some runouts or marginal placements.
Serious — runout; a fall risks injury.
A stretch with no protection below you (OpenBeta SafetyEnum).
Danger from the terrain itself, not fall distance.
Protection not yet assessed — the honest default.

rock_type.notes: Columnar jointing gives parallel crack systems; moderate drying.
Soft marine limestone (Dover, Beachy Head) — friable, specialist trad; never climb wet; protection unreliable.
Cobbles in matrix (Montserrat, Meteora, Riglos); pockety; protection often spaced.
Fair Head — grippy, dries reasonably; sea-cliff exposure.
Banded metamorphic (Alps, Norway); generally solid, good friction.
Dries fast; low seepage; good friction when cool. Poorly-cemented grades shed grains.
Coarse UK sandstone — superb friction, rounded breaks; dries fast, greasy in humidity; avoid when wet.
Seeps for days; overhangs stay wet; slick when humid.
Sound, fine-grained volcanic (Canaries/Teide); climbs like fine rhyolite; dries fast.
Hard, can be polished; variable drying.
Welsh mountain rock; lichenous, slow to dry high up.
Fragile when wet — do not climb wet (holds break). Highest dry friction.
Layered; can be friable and ledgy; holds moisture in breaks.
Non-porous — drains instantly but slick when wet; positive edges; quarried faces.

source.name: Mountain Project
source.teaches: multi-discipline route type, grade-system auto-detect from leading chars

source.name: multi-pitch.com
source.teaches: the curated route record + description style this schema is grounded in

source.name: OpenBeta
source.teaches: hierarchical areas, cascading gradeContext, all-systems grades, composable disciplines, structured pitches

source.name: theCrag
source.teaches: grade context at any level, structured cascading tag-sets, 0–100 quality → stars

source.name: UKClimbing
source.teaches: faceted crag search (rocktype/aspect/type), first-class access notes

sun_window.meaning: Sun from around midday (roughly W-facing) — cold starts, warm finishes.
Open to the sun most of the day (roughly S-facing) — dries fast, hot in summer.
Sun until around midday (roughly E-facing) — cool afternoons.
Little or no direct sun (N-facing or sheltered) — slow to dry, cool in heat.
